arXiv:2003.13028v1  [cs.CL]  29 Mar 2020

Abstractive Summarization with Combination of Pre-trained Sequence-to-Sequence and Saliency Models∗

Itsumi Saito, Kyosuke Nishida, Kosuke Nishida, Junji Tomita NTT Media Intelligence Laboratory, NTT Corporation itsumi.saito.df@hco.ntt.co.

7180c258-3133-4dc7-907c-2e461e5c0cb6.label: text_chunk
7180c258-3133-4dc7-907c-2e461e5c0cb6.text:
jp

Abstract

Pre-trained sequence-to-sequence (seq-to-seq) models have significantly improved the accu- racy of several language generation tasks, in- cluding abstractive summarization. Although the fluency of abstractive summarization has been greatly improved by fine-tuning these models, it is not clear whether they can also identify the important parts of the source text to be included in the summary.

dcc1d95f-5676-46d2-b2cb-d2a6c8b6a97c.label: text_chunk
dcc1d95f-5676-46d2-b2cb-d2a6c8b6a97c.text: In this study, we investigated the effectiveness of combin- ing saliency models that identify the impor- tant parts of the source text with the pre- trained seq-to-seq models through extensive experiments. We also proposed a new com- bination model consisting of a saliency model that extracts a token sequence from a source text and a seq-to-seq model that takes the se- quence as an additional input text.

4717a15c-ae41-4790-bc9c-172523c3e7a3.label: text_chunk
4717a15c-ae41-4790-bc9c-172523c3e7a3.text: Experimen- tal results showed that most of the combina- tion models outperformed a simple fine-tuned seq-to-seq model on both the CNN/DM and XSum datasets even if the seq-to-seq model is pre-trained on large-scale corpora. Moreover, for the CNN/DM dataset, the proposed com- bination model exceeded the previous best- performed model by 1.33 points on ROUGE- L.

0143ba20-cf1e-4467-b6f2-6c23821bd040.label: text_chunk
0143ba20-cf1e-4467-b6f2-6c23821bd040.text:
1 Introduction

Pre-trained language models such as BERT ( De- vlin et al. , 2019 ) have significantly improved the accuracy of various language processing tasks. However, we cannot apply BERT to language gen- eration tasks as is because its model structure is not suitable for language generation. Several pre-trained seq-to-seq models for language gen- eration ( Lewis et al. , 2019 ; Raffel et al.

4a93f5c2-9cd2-4298-9451-6bab1918700e.label: text_chunk
4a93f5c2-9cd2-4298-9451-6bab1918700e.text:
, 2019 ) based on an encoder-decoder Transformer model, which is a standard model for language genera- tion, have recently been proposed. These mod-
els have achieved state-of-the-art results in vari- ous language generation tasks, including abstrac- tive summarization.However, when generating a summary, it is es- sential to correctly predict which part of the source text should be included in the summary.

e1d04a0b-5f2d-4d14-8072-b687602a5496.label: text_chunk
e1d04a0b-5f2d-4d14-8072-b687602a5496.text: Some previous studies without pre-training have exam- ined combining extractive summarization with ab- stractive summarization ( Gehrmann et al. , 2018 ; Hsu et al. , 2018 ). Although pre-trained seq-to-seq models have achieved higher accuracy compared to previous models, it is not clear whether model- ing “Which part of the source text is important?” can be learned through pre-training.

3074549b-c6e9-4e58-a208-5e7bf23aab69.label: text_chunk
3074549b-c6e9-4e58-a208-5e7bf23aab69.text: The purpose of this study is to clarify the effec- tiveness of combining saliency models that iden- tify the important part of the source text with a pre- trained seq-to-seq model in the abstractive sum- marization task.

b51f384f-305d-4fdd-852b-9db016b7615b.label: text_chunk
b51f384f-305d-4fdd-852b-9db016b7615b.text:
Our main contributions are as follows:

• We investigated nine combinations of pre- trained seq-to-seq and token-level saliency models,

 where the saliency models share the parameters with the encoder of the seq-to-seq model or extract important tokens indepen- dently of the encoder.

c9399f11-f98b-4f0c-ab3a-c19b469c3536.label: text_chunk
c9399f11-f98b-4f0c-ab3a-c19b469c3536.text:
• We proposed a new combination model,

 the conditional summarization model with im- portant tokens ( CIT ), in which a token se- quence extracted by a saliency model is ex- plicitly given to a seq-to-seq model as an ad- ditional input text.

• We evaluated the combination models on the CNN/DM ( Hermann et al. ,

 2015 ) and XSum ( Narayan et al.

6e717269-e1aa-438e-b4ad-55f93b59803d.label: text_chunk
6e717269-e1aa-438e-b4ad-55f93b59803d.text:
, 2018 ) datasets. Our CIT model outperformed a simple fine-tuned model in terms of ROUGE scores on both datasets.∗ Work in progress.

2 Task Deﬁnition

Our study focuses on two tasks: abstractive sum- marization and saliency detection. The main task is abstractive summarization and the sub task is saliency detection, which is the prediction of im- portant parts of the source text.

53bfd1d6-c4e9-493a-a346-008f227fa30f.label: text_chunk
53bfd1d6-c4e9-493a-a346-008f227fa30f.text:
The problem for- mulations of each task are described below.

Task 1 (Abstractive summarization) Given the source text X , the output is an abstractive sum- mary Y = ( y 1 , . . . , y T ) . Task 2 (Saliency detection) Given the source text X with L words X = ( x 1 , . . .

f95a4137-729d-493c-86b2-bb5762edb740.label: text_chunk
f95a4137-729d-493c-86b2-bb5762edb740.text:
, x L ) , the output is the saliency score S = { S 1 , S 2 , ...S L } .

In this study, we investigate several combina- tions of models for these two tasks.

3 Pre-trained seq-to-seq Model

There are several pre-trained seq-to-seq models applied for abstractive summarization ( Song et al. , 2019 ; Dong et al.

2171f655-4f0b-4eda-87e4-935e1a1b1e36.label: text_chunk
2171f655-4f0b-4eda-87e4-935e1a1b1e36.text:
, 2019 ; Raffel et al. , 2019 ). The models use a simple Transformer-based encoder- decoder model ( Vaswani et al. , 2017 ) in which the encoder-decoder model is pre-trained on large un- labeled data.

3.1 Transformer-based Encoder-Decoder

In this work, we define the Transformer-based encoder-decoder model as follows.Encoder The encoder consists of M layer en- coder blocks.

213efd55-2efb-4578-a077-535cab365172.label: text_chunk
213efd55-2efb-4578-a077-535cab365172.text:
The input of the encoder is X = { x i , x 2 , ...x L } . The output through the M layer encoder blocks is defined as
H M e = { h M e 1 , h M e 2 , ...h M eL } ∈ R L × d . (1)


The encoder block consists of a self-attention module and a two-layer feed-forward network.Decoder The decoder consists of M layer de- coder blocks.

34fbe04f-532e-48be-8541-f09e4a56f252.label: text_chunk
34fbe04f-532e-48be-8541-f09e4a56f252.text:
The inputs of the decoder are the output of the encoder H M e and the output of the previous step of the decoder { y 1 , ..., y t − 1 } . The output through the M layer Transformer decoder blocks is defined as
H M d = { h M d 1 , ...

, h M dt } ∈ R t × d .

2ea8b486-f3d0-4473-813d-f1ea256f2477.label: text_chunk
2ea8b486-f3d0-4473-813d-f1ea256f2477.text:
(2)
In each step t , the h M dt is projected to the vocab- ulary space and the decoder outputs the highest probability token as the next token. The Trans- former decoder block consists of a self-attention module, a context-attention module, and a two- layer feed-forward network.

4f51fd94-8e80-42a9-99fb-35f6968a0de5.label: text_chunk
4f51fd94-8e80-42a9-99fb-35f6968a0de5.text: Multi-head Attention The encoder and de- coder blocks use multi-head attention, which con- sists of a combination of K attention heads and is denoted as Multihead( Q, K, V ) = Concat(head 1 , ..., head k ) W o , where each head is head i = Attention( QW Q i , KW K i , V W V i ) .

1aac80d0-6646-4a62-b40f-9491d7ab2bb2.label: text_chunk
1aac80d0-6646-4a62-b40f-9491d7ab2bb2.text:
The weight matrix A in each attention-head Attention( ˜ Q, ˜ K, ˜ V ) = A ˜ V is defined as
A = softmax ˜ Q ˜ K > √ d k ! ∈ R I × J , (3)
where d k = d/k , ˜ Q ∈ R I × d , ˜ K, ˜ V ∈ R J × d .

727b63b7-69bb-4ece-8d33-9945f3663504.label: text_chunk
727b63b7-69bb-4ece-8d33-9945f3663504.text: In the m -th layer of self-attention, the same rep- resentation H m · is given to Q , K , and V . In the context-attention, we give H m d to Q and H M e to K and V .

e7558e9b-502a-4257-a5dd-8dd2742c2aa5.label: text_chunk
e7558e9b-502a-4257-a5dd-8dd2742c2aa5.text:
3.2 Summary Loss Function

To fine-tune the seq-to-seq model for abstractive summarization, we use cross entropy loss as
L sum = − 1 N T N X n =1 T X t =1 log P ( y n t ) , (4)
where N is the number of training samples.

c320aace-c7ab-4551-9e43-cf72b76bdf39.label: text_chunk
c320aace-c7ab-4551-9e43-cf72b76bdf39.text:
4 Saliency Models

Several studies have proposed the combination of a token-level saliency model and a seq-to-seq model, which is not pre-trained, and reported its effectiveness ( Gehrmann et al. , 2018 ; Zhou et al. , 2017 ). We also use a simple token-level saliency model as a basic model in this study.

563f12ca-ff59-4f55-b086-62dc07ac52a6.label: text_chunk
563f12ca-ff59-4f55-b086-62dc07ac52a6.text:
4.1 Basic Saliency Model

A basic saliency model consists of M -layer Transformer encoder blocks ( Encoder sal ) and a single-layer feed-forward network. We define the saliency score of the l -th token ( 1 ≤ l ≤ L ) in the source text as
S l = σ ( W > 1 Encoder sal ( X ) l + b 1 ) , (5)

a9fe61dd-b450-4960-84a6-846e40c9b82a.label: text_chunk
a9fe61dd-b450-4960-84a6-846e40c9b82a.text:
where Encoder sal () represents the output of the last layer of Encoder sal , W 1 ∈ R d and b 1 are learnable parameters, and σ represents a sigmoid function.

4.2 Two Types of Saliency Model for Combination

In this study, we use two types of saliency model for combination: a shared encoder and an extrac- tor. Each model structure is based on the basic saliency model.

ac83991a-42cf-4154-b4f8-40b62df17196.label: text_chunk
ac83991a-42cf-4154-b4f8-40b62df17196.text:
We describe them below.Shared encoder The shared encoder shares the parameters of Encoder sal and the encoder of the seq-to-seq model. This model is jointly trained with the seq-to-seq model and the saliency score is used to bias the representations of the seq-to-seq model.

Extractor The extractor extracts the important tokens or sentences from the source text on the basis of the saliency score.

e02e236c-0b6b-4ddf-b753-c7897993f412.label: text_chunk
e02e236c-0b6b-4ddf-b753-c7897993f412.text:
The extractor is sep- arated with the seq-to-seq model, and each model is trained independently.

4.3 Pseudo Reference Label

The saliency model predicts the saliency score S l for each token x l . If there is a reference label r l ∈ { 0 , 1 } for each x l , we can train the saliency model in a supervised manner.

3c8b9541-e06f-4437-ae1f-23f6d2a04a0e.label: text_chunk
3c8b9541-e06f-4437-ae1f-23f6d2a04a0e.text:
However, the reference label for each token is typically not given, since the training data for the summarization consists of only the source text and its reference summary.

 Although there are no reference saliency labels, we can make pseudo reference labels by aligning both source and summary token sequences and ex- tracting common tokens ( Gehrmann et al. , 2018 ). We used pseudo labels when we train the saliency model in a supervised manner.

e338f392-fa52-49f0-944a-d87290ee633c.label: text_chunk
e338f392-fa52-49f0-944a-d87290ee633c.text:
4.4 Saliency Loss Function

To train the saliency model in a supervised way with pseudo reference labels, we use binary cross entropy loss as
L sal = − 1 N L N X n =1 L X l =1 r n l log S n l + (1 − r n l ) log(1 − S n l ) , (6)
where r n l is a pseudo reference label of token x l in the n -th sample.

51c00c2b-b4a2-47ba-a545-f5ef6023562e.label: text_chunk
51c00c2b-b4a2-47ba-a545-f5ef6023562e.text:
5 Combined Models

This section describes nine combinations of the pre-trained seq-to-seq model and saliency models.Combination types We roughly categorize the combinations into three types. Figure 1 shows an image of each combination.The first type uses the shared encoder ( § 5.1 ).

742abf60-daa5-4645-b44d-a20609422a33.label: text_chunk
742abf60-daa5-4645-b44d-a20609422a33.text:
These models consist of the shared encoder and the decoder, where the shared encoder module plays two roles: saliency detection and the encod- ing of the seq-to-seq model.

 The saliency scores are used to bias the representation of the seq-to- seq model for several models in this type.The second type uses the extractor ( § 5.2 , § 5.3 ).

59f92ddb-90f4-471b-b548-cab855917a91.label: text_chunk
59f92ddb-90f4-471b-b548-cab855917a91.text:
These models consist of the extractor, encoder, and decoder and follow two steps: first, the ex- tractor extracts the important tokens or sentences from the source text, and second, the encoder uses them as an input of the seq-to-seq models.

 Our proposed model (CIT) belongs to this type.The third type uses both the shared encoder and the extractor ( § 5.4 ).

668a818d-5788-4cd2-9d6c-6c9e5674e33e.label: text_chunk
668a818d-5788-4cd2-9d6c-6c9e5674e33e.text:
These models consist of the extractor, shared encoder, and decoder and also follow two steps: first, the extractor extracts the important tokens from the source text, and second, the shared encoder uses them as an input of the seq-to-seq model.

Loss function From the viewpoint of the loss function, there are two major types of model: those that use the saliency loss ( § 4.4 ) and those that do not.

9e78dc3b-180a-4f9b-aead-eec25b93a92f.label: text_chunk
9e78dc3b-180a-4f9b-aead-eec25b93a92f.text:
We also denote the loss function for the seq-to-seq model as L abs and the loss function for the extractor as L ext . L ext is trained with L sal , and L abs is trained with L sum or L sum + L sal .

5.1 Using Shared Encoder to Combine the Saliency Model and the Seq-to-seq Model

Multi-Task (MT) This model trains the shared encoder and the decoder by minimizing both the summary and saliency losses.

59f8c0b3-cb4c-4939-a5a4-baf7964b4087.label: text_chunk
59f8c0b3-cb4c-4939-a5a4-baf7964b4087.text:
The loss function of this model is L abs = L sum + L sal .Selective Encoding (SE) This model uses the saliency score to weight the shared encoder out- put. Specifically, the final output h M el of the shared encoder is weighted as
˜ h M el = h M el S l . (7)


Then, we replace the input of the decoder h M el with ˜ h M el . Although Zhou et al.

0c63c272-ef7d-47c2-9573-c95346384c26.label: text_chunk
0c63c272-ef7d-47c2-9573-c95346384c26.text:
( 2017 ) used BiGRU, we use Transformer for fair comparison. The loss function of this model is L abs = L sum .Figure 1: Combinations of seq-to-seq and saliency models. Purple : Encoder. Blue : Decoder.

 Red : Shared encoder, which is a shared model for saliency detection and encoding, used in (a), (b), and (e).

b82c0f2e-ae68-4a32-b297-9066e8c11625.label: text_chunk
b82c0f2e-ae68-4a32-b297-9066e8c11625.text: Yellow : Extractor, which is an independent saliency model to extract important (c) sentences X s or (d), (e) tokens C from the source text X . Each of these colored blocks represents M -layer Transformer blocks. Gray : Linear transformation. Green : Context attention.

fb82a87a-cfbc-4520-932e-3438783115ee.label: text_chunk
fb82a87a-cfbc-4520-932e-3438783115ee.text: Pink : Output trained in a supervised manner, where S is the saliency score and Y is the summary.Combination of SE and MT This model has the same structure as the SE. The loss function of this model is L abs = L sum + L sal .Selective Attention (SA) This model weights the attention scores of the decoder side, unlike the SE model.

7d1aa6ca-83bd-405c-ba33-e315d0aa7d4f.label: text_chunk
7d1aa6ca-83bd-405c-ba33-e315d0aa7d4f.text:
Specifically, the attention score a t i ∈ R L in each step t is weighted by S l .

 a t i is a t -th row of A i ∈ R T × L , which is a weight matrix of the i -th attention head in the context-attention (Eq. ( 3 )).˜ a t il = a t il S l P l a t il S l . (8)
Gehrmann et al.

23cc9c4d-df35-4442-9289-a63535a21b22.label: text_chunk
23cc9c4d-df35-4442-9289-a63535a21b22.text:
( 2018 ) took a similar approach in that their model weights the copy probability of a pointer-generator model.

 However, as the pre-trained seq-to-seq model does not have a copy mechanism, we weight the context-attention for all Transformer decoder blocks. The loss function of this model is L abs = L sum .Combination of SA and MT This model has the same structure as the SA.

17cb7e81-9e0c-4812-890e-e66d4f151248.label: text_chunk
17cb7e81-9e0c-4812-890e-e66d4f151248.text:
The loss function of this model is L abs = L sum + L sal .

5.2 Using the Extractor to Reﬁne the Input Text

Sentence Extraction then Generation (SEG) This model first extracts the saliency sentences on the basis of a sentence-level saliency score S j .

05b52d39-6861-498e-91f2-29cad472dd23.label: text_chunk
05b52d39-6861-498e-91f2-29cad472dd23.text:
S j is calculated by using the token level saliency score of the extractor, S l , as
S j = 1 N j X l : x l ∈ X j S l , (9)
where N j and X j are the number of tokens and the set of tokens within the j -th sentence.

 Top P sentences are extracted according to the sentence- level saliency score and then concatenated as one text X s .

6c1094cb-ac2b-45f8-9f33-799e2aca68fd.label: text_chunk
6c1094cb-ac2b-45f8-9f33-799e2aca68fd.text:
These extracted sentences are then used as the input of the seq-to-seq model.In the training, we extracted X s , which max- imizes the ROUGE-L scores with the reference summary text. In the test, we used the average number of sentences in X s in the training set as P .

 The loss function of the extractor is L ext = L sal , and that of the seq-to-seq model is L abs = L sum .

5ca9ce56-df95-4afe-ae3a-b5e035b55488.label: text_chunk
5ca9ce56-df95-4afe-ae3a-b5e035b55488.text:
5.3 Proposed: Using Extractor to Extract an Additional Input Text

Conditional Summarization Model with Im- portant Tokens We propose a new combination of the extractor and the seq-to-seq model, CIT, which can consider important tokens explicitly. Although the SE and SA models softly weight the representations of the source text or attention scores, they cannot select salient tokens explicitly.

660a51ab-c3f5-44f7-a2cf-fb795c55b5f1.label: text_chunk
660a51ab-c3f5-44f7-a2cf-fb795c55b5f1.text: SEG explicitly extracts the salient sentences from the source text, but it cannot give token-level infor- mation to the seq-to-seq model, and it sometimes drops important information when extracting sen- tences. In contrast, CIT uses the tokens extracted according to saliency scores as an additional input of the seq-to-seq model. By adding token-level in- formation, CIT can effectively guide the abstrac- tive summary without dropping any important in- formation.

6c6ba708-5884-4967-85dc-df6c569693c7.label: text_chunk
6c6ba708-5884-4967-85dc-df6c569693c7.text: Specifically, K tokens C = { c 1 , ..., c K } are ex- tracted in descending order of the saliency score S . S is obtained by inputting X to the extractor.The order of C retains the order of the source text X . A combined text ˜ X = Concat( C, X ) is given to the seq-to-seq model as the input text.

8c404385-5129-480c-8335-7ea6df8aa3d2.label: text_chunk
8c404385-5129-480c-8335-7ea6df8aa3d2.text:
The loss function of the extractor is L ext = L sal , and that of the seq-to-seq model is L abs = L sum .

5.4 Proposed: Combination of Extractor and Shared Encoder

Combination of CIT and SE This model com- bines the CIT and SE, so CIT uses an extractor for extracting important tokens, and SE is trained by using a shared encoder in the seq-to-seq model. The SE model is trained in an unsupervised way.

3ea5b332-3a5b-4855-af78-2efab7080a16.label: text_chunk
3ea5b332-3a5b-4855-af78-2efab7080a16.text:
The output H M e ∈ R L + K of the shared encoder is weighted by saliency score S ∈ R L + K with Eq.

 ( 7 ), where S is estimated by using the output of the shared encoder with Eq. ( 5 ).

2dcaa07f-665b-4666-afba-9155b5fa0040.label: text_chunk
2dcaa07f-665b-4666-afba-9155b5fa0040.text:
The loss func- tion of the extractor is L ext = L sal , and that of the seq-to-seq model is L abs = L sum .Combination of CIT and SA This model com- bines the CIT and SA, so we also train two saliency models.

 The SA model is trained in an unsupervised way, the same as the CIT + SE model.

ab644b23-1563-46ba-9865-a3c786c18a0e.label: text_chunk
ab644b23-1563-46ba-9865-a3c786c18a0e.text:
The attention score a t i ∈ R L + K is weighted by S ∈ R L + K with Eq. ( 8 ). The loss function of the extractor is L ext = L sal , and that of the seq-to-seq model is L abs = L sum .

6 Experiments

6.1 Dataset

We used the CNN/DM dataset ( Hermann et al. , 2015 ) and the XSum dataset ( Narayan et al.

e35a37ba-8997-44c4-8bc2-5a50b06c8903.label: text_chunk
e35a37ba-8997-44c4-8bc2-5a50b06c8903.text:
, 2018 ), which are both standard datasets for news summarization. The details of the two datasets are listed in Table 1 . The CNN/DM is a highly ex- tractive summarization dataset and the XSum is a highly abstractive summarization dataset.

6.1.1 Model Conﬁgurations

We used BART LARGE ( Lewis et al.

1e4a59d2-88ed-4a2b-841c-3f582074a664.label: text_chunk
1e4a59d2-88ed-4a2b-841c-3f582074a664.text: , 2019 ), which is one of the state-of-the-art models, as the pre- trained seq-to-seq model and RoBERTa BASE ( Liu et al. , 2019 ) as the initial model of the extractor. In the extractor of CIT, stop words and duplicate tokens are ignored for the XSum dataset.We used fairseq 1 for the implementation of the seq-to-seq model.

6.2 Evaluation Metrics

We used ROUGE scores (F1), including ROUGE- 1 (R-1), ROUGE-2 (R-2), and ROUGE-L (R-L), as the evaluation metrics ( Lin , 2004 ). ROUGE scores were calculated using the files2rouge toolkit 3 .

6.3 Results

Do saliency models improve summarization ac- curacy in highly extractive datasets?

7 Related Work and Discussion

Pre-trained Language Models for Abstractive Summarization Liu ( 2019 ) used BERT for their sentence-level extractive summarization model. Zhang et al.

b9f2b9cd-e6df-4647-b89e-36fbf7bab71c.label: text_chunk
b9f2b9cd-e6df-4647-b89e-36fbf7bab71c.text:
( 2019b ) proposed a new pre-trained model that considers document-level informa- tion for sentence-level extractive summarization. Several researchers have published pre-trained encoder-decoder models very recently ( Wang et al. , 2019 ; Lewis et al. , 2019 ; Raffel et al.

 , 2019 ). Wang et al. ( 2019 ) pre-trained a Transformer- based pointer-generator model.

8c6e4de6-bb33-4bb5-991f-aa13032e6f16.label: text_chunk
8c6e4de6-bb33-4bb5-991f-aa13032e6f16.text: Lewis et al. ( 2019 ) pre-trained a standard Transformer-based encoder-decoder model using large unlabeled data and achieved state-of-the-art results. Dong et al. ( 2019 ) and Xiao et al. ( 2020 ) extended the BERT structure to handle seq-to-seq tasks.

0b62e5b2-903a-46b6-8cb3-2bf63ee42ef2.label: text_chunk
0b62e5b2-903a-46b6-8cb3-2bf63ee42ef2.text: All the studies above focused on how to learn a universal pre-trained model; they did not consider the combination of pre-trained and saliency mod- els for an abstractive summarization model.Abstractive Summarization with Saliency Models Hsu et al. ( 2018 ), Gehrmann et al. ( 2018 ), and You et al.

1ed4c53d-1ed6-47e3-b813-481c8cae7489.label: text_chunk
1ed4c53d-1ed6-47e3-b813-481c8cae7489.text:
( 2019 ) incorporated a sentence- and word-level extractive model in the pointer-generator model.

 Their models weight the copy probability for the source text by using an extractive model and guide the pointer-generator model to copy important words. Li et al. ( 2018 ) proposed a keyword guided abstractive sum- marization model.

55fce6ca-dae3-4f2d-be20-0f19c33dad96.label: text_chunk
55fce6ca-dae3-4f2d-be20-0f19c33dad96.text:
Chen and Bansal ( 2018 ) proposed a sentence extraction and re-writing model that is trained in an end-to-end manner
by using reinforcement learning. Cao et al. ( 2018 ) proposed a search and rewrite model.

 Mendes et al. ( 2019 ) proposed a combination of sentence-level extraction and compression. None of these models are based on a pre-trained model.

51d7f1dd-17d8-49f9-a496-a5441c4815be.label: text_chunk
51d7f1dd-17d8-49f9-a496-a5441c4815be.text:
In contrast, our purpose is to clarify whether combined models are effective or not, and we are the first to investigate the combination of pre-trained seq-to-seq and saliency models. We compared a variety of combinations and clarified which combination is the most effective.

8 Conclusion

This is the first study that has conducted extensive experiments to investigate the effectiveness of in- corporating saliency models into the pre-trained seq-to-seq model.

726f6685-a680-4479-9ca8-b1157b7066a9.label: text_chunk
726f6685-a680-4479-9ca8-b1157b7066a9.text:
From the results, we found that saliency models were effective in finding im- portant parts of the source text, even if the seq- to-seq model is pre-trained on large-scale cor- pora, especially for generating an highly extrac- tive summary.

 We also proposed a new combi- nation model, CIT, that outperformed simple fine- tuning and other combination models.

2643fa75-b68a-4544-b0a6-c42fc7f070c0.label: text_chunk
2643fa75-b68a-4544-b0a6-c42fc7f070c0.text:
Our combi- nation model improved the summarization accu- racy without any additional pre-training data and can be applied to any pre-trained model.

 While re- cent studies have been conducted to improve sum- marization accuracy by increasing the amount of pre-training data and developing new pre-training strategies, this study sheds light on the importance of saliency models in abstractive summarization.
